title: Optimization Using Locally-Quantum Decoders
authors: Noah Shutty, Avijit Mandal, Seyoon Ragavan, Quentin Buzet, André Chailloux, Nicholas C. Rubin, Abid Khan, Sami Boulebnane, Ruslan Shaydulin, John Azariah, Stephen P. Jordan
abstract: It was pointed out in [JSW+25] that widely-studied optimization problems such as D-regular max-k-XORSAT can be reduced to decoding of LDPC codes, using quantum algorithms related to Regev's reduction. LDPC codes have very good decoders, such as Belief Propagation (BP), and this therefore makes D-regular max-k-XORSAT an enticing target for this class of quantum algorithms. However, BP was found insufficient to achieve quantum advantage. Here, we develop an intrinsically quantum decoding technique, which decodes classical LDPC codes subject to coherent superpositions of bit flip errors. For average-case instances of D-regular max-k-XORSAT drawn from Gallager's ensemble, this quantum decoder strongly outperforms classical belief propagation at many values of k and D. For some (k,D) the approximate optima achievable using this decoder surpass both Prange's algorithm and simulated annealing. However, we stop short of achieving quantum advantage because we identify an enhancement to Prange's algorithm that recovers a precise tie, much as a precise tie was observed between the standard version of Prange's algorithm and a more limited version of locally-quantum decoding in [CT24].
summary: This paper introduces a new quantum decoding technique designed to solve combinatorial optimization problems like max-k-XORSAT. By tailoring measurements to the specific structure of LDPC codes, the authors achieve performance that beats standard classical algorithms like Belief Propagation and Simulated Annealing for certain problem instances. While the authors identify a classical heuristic that can match their results, the work demonstrates a promising direction for using quantum-enhanced decoding to tackle hard optimization tasks.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop an efficient quantum decoding technique to solve the D-regular max-k-XORSAT optimization problem and investigate if it can achieve quantum advantage over classical algorithms.
main_result: The authors develop a locally-quantum decoding technique that outperforms classical Belief Propagation and can surpass Prange's algorithm and simulated annealing for certain parameters, though they find a classical enhancement (Turbo Prange) that matches their performance.
method: The method uses Regev's reduction to map optimization to LDPC decoding and implements a code-dependent Fine-Grained Unambiguous Measurement (FGUM) strategy involving local basis changes on qubits.
model_or_system: The problem is modeled using D-regular max-k-XORSAT instances drawn from Gallager's ensemble, represented as bipartite Tanner graphs with bit-flip errors occurring in coherent superposition.
key_observables: The fraction of satisfied constraints (optimization score) and the error rate/failure probability of the quantum decoder.
important_parameters: The constraint arity (k), the variable degree (D), the bit-flip error rate (alpha), and the QAOA circuit depth (p).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on average-case instances from the Gallager ensemble and assumes a high-girth/tree-like graph structure in the large n limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a Tanner graph structure; Table 1 compares optimization scores across different algorithms (Prange, Simulated Annealing, DQI+BP, Regev+FGUM).
paper_structure: The paper identifies the limitations of previous quantum decoding approaches, proposes a new code-dependent FGUM technique, provides mathematical proofs for error bounds and statistical closeness, and concludes with a performance comparison against classical and quantum benchmarks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A Tanner graph from the (2, 3) Gallager ensemble. Highlighted in blue is a set of parity check nodes such that each bit node is connected by an edge to exactly one check from this set.
